nativeRange.habitat: The Schooling Bannerfish is found along outer reef slopes and in current-swept channels, typically at depths of roughly 5 to 30 m, though it has been recorded as deep as 210 m. Juveniles tend to gather around isolated patch reefs, while adults are commonly seen forming large schools in open water well above the reef bottom.

diet.notes: A natural plankton feeder. Generally accepts a varied diet of meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and finely chopped seafood, alongside frozen or prepared planktivore formulas. Feeding several small meals per day is commonly advised given the species' high activity level and small mouth.

goodWith: other Heniochus diphreutes if introduced simultaneously

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Joao D'Andretta, 2048x1235. Sharp, vibrant school of 6+ over a coral reef showing the classic schooling habit, bold black-and-white bars, yellow dorsal/anal fins and long white dorsal filaments.
Photo by Jean-Paul Boerekamps, 2048x1536. Crisp side-profile individual in perfect frame with a whole school behind it; clearly shows the white dorsal filament, black bars and yellow fins.
Photo by portioid, 2048x1931. Sharp single-fish close-up detailing the yellow dorsal/tail, black bars and streaming white filament; kept as a non-recommended detail shot.

summary: A peaceful schooling butterflyfish often confused with the Moorish Idol, sporting bold black-and-white bars and a long white dorsal filament. Generally considered one of the more reef-tolerant Heniochus and tends to do best in groups with ample open swimming space.
careNotes:
Heniochus diphreutes closely resembles Heniochus acuminatus and is sometimes called the False Moorish Idol for its similarity to Zanclus cornutus. It tends to be distinguished from H. acuminatus by a shorter snout, a more rounded body, and a more strongly schooling habit. Whereas H. acuminatus is typically observed in pairs, H. diphreutes is well-documented forming large schools, especially as adults — keepers generally report best results when keeping three or more individuals introduced together.

This species is generally considered the most reef-tolerant member of the genus, with a stronger preference for zooplankton over coral polyps. Most reefkeepers report it leaves stony corals — both small-polyp (SPS) and large-polyp (LPS) varieties — alone, though caution is still warranted around small ornamental shrimp, feather dusters, and clam mantles. It is generally regarded as reef safe with caution.

Juveniles have been documented acting as cleaner fish at reef cleaning stations, picking parasites from larger fish — a behavior also reported, less frequently, in adults.
breedingNotes: Spawning in captivity has been very rarely documented. In the wild, the species is egg-scattering (oviparous), with pairs forming during the spawning season and releasing eggs that drift freely in open water. Because the eggs and larvae develop in open-ocean conditions, successfully rearing them in a home aquarium is considered extremely difficult.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaetodontidae / Acanthuriformes), adult size (max 21 cm TL), depth and temperature range (preferred 19.1–28.1°C, mean 26.1°C), native range across the Indo-Pacific, planktivorous diet, and the species' strongly schooling habit in adults.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner; successful captive breeding in home aquariums has rarely if ever been documented. Wild pairs spawn during the breeding season, releasing pelagic eggs.
